title: Dynamic Market Making with Asymmetric Information and Market Power
abstract: We study the dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread using a multiperiod trading model with oligopolistic market makers. Traders smooth out their trading even though they are not strategic, and thus trading persists after the arrival of information or liquidity shocks. Traders act quickly on their private information while postponing hedging trades until later periods. The market power of market makers enables them to widen bid-ask spreads when trading incentives are heightened. Consequently, both trading volume and bid-ask spread may exhibit U-shaped patterns. Our model calibration aligns with the empirical intraday patterns observed in bid-ask spreads and trading volumes. (JELD53, D61, D82, G12, G14, G18)

author.Wen Chen: Rawls College of Business, Texas Tech University,United Sates

article.1 The Model:
Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

The existing empirical literature has documented U-shaped intraday patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spread across major stock exchanges. Specifically, studies have shown that the average trading volume tends to peak at the beginning and end of the trading day, with a decline during the middle hours.1Similarly, bid-ask spreads are observed to be highest at market opening and closure and lowest during the middle of the trading duration.2The simultaneous occurrence of U-shaped patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spreads has been puzzling. Existing theories have not been able to explain why these double U-shaped patterns can arise endogenously. In this paper, we present a dynamic trading model based on flows of private information and liquidity shocks with imperfectly competitive liquidity suppliers. The model provides a unified and coherent explanation for the observed U-shaped patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spread and sheds light on the underlying mechanisms that drive these intriguing intraday patterns in financial markets.

As new information or liquidity shocks arrive, traders engage in speculative trading based on their private information or hedge against the risk from liquidity shocks. Market makers, as liquidity suppliers, may possess market power due to imperfect competition. This market power of market makers leads other traders to smooth out their trading, even though these traders are not strategic. Therefore, the market power of market makers dampens and spreads out trading spikes due to either new information arrivals or new liquidity shocks, and thus trading persists even after the arrival of information and liquidity shocks. When both information and liquidity shocks arrive at a constant rate, traders tend to trade more aggressively on their private information during earlier periods and postpone hedging trades to later periods. Consequently, trading volume may exhibit a U-shaped pattern, with higher trading volume observed at the beginning and end of the trading duration and lower volume in the middle. The market power of liquidity suppliers enables them to widen bid-ask spreads when trading incentives are heightened, resulting in wider bid-ask spreads at market opening and closure.3

Our model considers a market with three types of investors: informed investors, uninformed investors, and market makers (who are also uninformed). Informed and uninformed traders are perfectly competitive, while market makers have imperfect competition. At each trading periodt=0,1,…,T⁠, traders can trade a risk-free asset and a risky security to maximize their expected constant absolute risk-averse (CARA) utility from the terminal wealth at periodT+ 1. Informed investors receive private signals about the terminal payoff of the security, motivating them to trade based on their private information. Additionally, informed investors experience non-information-based trading incentives, termed liquidity shocks, which we model as random endowments of nontradable assets. These nontradable risky assets’ payoffs are correlated with stock payoffs and can only be liquidated at periodT+ 1. Consequently, informed investors also have trading demand driven by their liquidity needs for hedging.

At each trading period, traders can sell to market makers at the bid price, buy from them at the ask price, or choose not to trade at all. Market makers, in turn, optimize their price and quantity decisions, determining their bid and ask quantities simultaneously. They take into account the inverse supply function (a function of the market makers’ purchasing quantity) of all other participants and the inverse demand function (a function of the market makers’ selling quantity) of all other participants. As a result, the bid and ask prices set by market makers are strategically determined to achieve their optimal trading quantities.

In practice, market makers often engage in both agency and principal market-making activities. However, allowing market makers to simultaneously perform both types of market making would render our dynamic model highly intractable. To maintain tractability while still capturing essential market dynamics, we have opted to examine two distinct scenarios. In the first scenario, we consider agency trading market makers who offset trades and maximize the expected profit from each trading round without carrying inventory over time. In the second scenario, we analyze principal trading market makers who actively manage inventory over time to maximize their expected total profits.

By examining these simplified scenarios, we can effectively reduce the dimensionality of the model, enabling us to analytically solve for the equilibrium endogenous bid and ask prices and depths. This approach allows us to gain meaningful insights and understand the factors influencing the intraday patterns of trading volume and bid-ask spread. Remarkably, we find that the majority of our main results remain qualitatively similar in both cases. Traders react promptly to their private information while deferring hedging trades until later periods, leading to potential U-shaped patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spread.

To better understand the potential U-shaped patterns in trading volume, we examine the distinct effects of information and hedging on their dynamics in two scenarios. We assume that both private information and liquidity shocks are present at market opening to ensure that the equilibrium price is not fully revealing. In the first case, where only information arrives after market opening, as more information arrives over time, both uninformed traders and market makers become less uncertain about the fundamental value of the asset. Consequently, the discrepancy between the reservation prices of informed and uninformed traders diminishes,4leading to a decline in trading volume over time. In contrast, in the second case, where only liquidity shocks occur after market opening, as more liquidity shocks arrive over time, the discrepancy in reservation prices increases, resulting in higher trading volume in later periods.

When both information and liquidity shocks arrive at constant rates, trading volume tends to concentrate at the market opening and closure. Traders are motivated to trade on their private information in earlier periods to capitalize on potential profits, as other traders might also act on similar information. However, they delay their hedging trades to later periods, as the adverse selection effect becomes less severe over time. Holding the competitiveness of market makers constant, the U-shaped pattern of trading volume becomes more pronounced when the volatility of hedging demand increases or when informed traders’ private information is noisier. This occurs because high volatility of hedging demand leads uninformed traders to perceive that trades are primarily driven by hedging needs. Consequently, the adverse selection effect becomes less pronounced, allowing informed traders to trade more aggressively on their private information at market opening and shift more trading due to hedging demand at market close. As a result, the U-shaped pattern of trading volume becomes more pronounced.

In the scenario involving new arrivals of liquidity shocks (with or without new arrivals of information), an important distinction arises between agency trading and principal trading market makers. In the case of agency trading market makers, who do not carry inventory and aim to maximize profit in each trading round by offsetting trades with other traders, the trading volume exhibits a hump-shaped pattern several periods before market closure. This hump-shaped pattern arises because traders anticipate wider bid-ask spreads toward market closure and, to reduce total trading costs, optimally shift some of their trading to earlier periods when bid-ask spreads are relatively lower.5Conversely, in the case of principal trading market makers, the expected trading volume tends to increase as market closure approaches, without exhibiting a hump-shaped pattern beforehand. This difference is primarily because of the principal market makers’ objective to maximize total trading profits over the entire trading duration. They determine their optimal trading quantities (bid depth and ask depth) based on traders’ demand schedules at each trading period, which in turn dictate the trading prices. The expected bid-ask spreads set by principal market makers in earlier periods are relatively higher than those set by agency market makers. As a result, traders continue to spread out their trading over time but shift less of their trading to earlier periods. A larger volume of trading occurs toward market closure when market makers can also set wider spreads, as it is the last opportunity to trade. Therefore, the trading volume does not exhibit a hump-shaped pattern.

Our model generates several testable empirical predictions. For example, it predicts that during periods of news events, the variation in trading volume tends to be smaller for stocks with less competitive liquidity suppliers. The U-shaped pattern of the bid-ask spread tends to be more pronounced for stocks with less competitive liquidity suppliers (e.g., small stocks) and stocks with higher order imbalance, allowing liquidity suppliers to have greater market power. Moreover, the U-shaped pattern of the trading volume tends to be more pronounced for stocks with lower information asymmetry or a higher proportion of liquidity traders, holding all other factors constant.

Our main findings are applicable to markets characterized by imperfect competition among liquidity suppliers, where some liquidity providers, such as market makers, have market power. As an illustrative example, we perform model calibration using stocks listed on NASDAQ, which is one of the most active stock trading venues in the United States in terms of volume and market capitalization of shares traded. Previous studies (Biais, Bisière, and Spatt 2010) suggest that competition among market makers on NASDAQ remains imperfect, even with the introduction of electronic markets.

Specifically, we calibrate the model parameters to fit the empirical intraday U-shaped patterns of bid-ask spreads and trading volume using TAQ data from January 2000 to December 2021. The theoretical expected bid-ask spreads and trading volumes, as predicted by our calibrated model, generally align with the corresponding empirical values at half-hour intervals throughout the trading day. Notably, the calibrated parameter values indicate a higher precision of private information at market opening compared to other periods. Conversely, the uncertainty associated with hedging needs is found to be higher near market closure in our calibration results. This suggests that there tends to be more information arrival at market opening, while a greater demand for hedging emerges near market closure compared to other periods throughout the trading day. The calibration results further validate the relevance and applicability of our model in capturing the intraday patterns observed in real-world trading environments, especially in markets with imperfectly competitive liquidity suppliers.

Major financial markets across the globe, including the NYSE, NASDAQ, LSE, and Euronext, operate as hybrid models where both market makers and limit orders coexist. In these markets, designated market makers or dealers play a crucial role in facilitating trading. They actively maintain two-sided markets during trading hours, standing ready to buy and sell at the displayed bids and offers. As a result, they fulfill a fundamental role as essential liquidity providers in these markets.6For specific smaller stocks and periods marked by relatively low liquidity, investors predominantly trade through market makers (Anand and Venkataraman 2016). In quote-driven electronic markets, such as the Stock Exchange Automated Quotation (SEAQ) system used for trading small-cap LSE stocks, traders often need to trade through market makers as direct trading among themselves is typically not feasible. This highlights the relevance of our model in financial markets where designated market makers play a crucial role in facilitating trades.7

Competition among market makers in many financial markets is far from being perfect. For instance,Christie and Schultz (1994)suggest that NASDAQ dealers might engage in implicit collusion to maintain wide spreads. Although NASDAQ transitioned away from a quote-driven market in 1997, allowing all participants to submit limit orders to its central limit order book, the competition among liquidity providers remains imperfect. This is evident in the study conducted byBiais, Bisière, and Spatt (2010). They analyze trades and order placements on both NASDAQ and a competing electronic order book, Island. Their findings reveal that even with the introduction of electronic markets, competition among market makers in these markets remains imperfect. They emphasize that according to conversations with market participants, only a limited number of large professional traders actively monitored Island and NASDAQ and strategically undercut NASDAQ quotes to earn trading profits, resulting in imperfect competition. Moreover, they point out “market makers’ quotes were the main source of liquidity on NASDAQ. These quotes were disseminated electronically on the NASDAQ screens.”Hua, Kong, and Wang (2023)show that the magnitude of trading volume spikes at market closure has monotonically increased over time, consistent with the observation that institutional traders’ rebalancing needs at market closure have risen in recent years. Additionally, the decrease in the magnitude of bid-ask spread spikes from 2011-2015 to 2016-2021 suggests that the market power of market makers may have decreased during this period. Nevertheless, the double U-shaped pattern in bid-ask spreads and trading volume (turnover) remains consistent throughout the entire sample period from 2000 to 2021, as documented in their paper. This pattern indicates that competition among liquidity suppliers on NASDAQ is still not perfect, allowing market makers to increase the bid-ask spread in response to heightened trading incentives.

Our model is relevant for markets in which certain liquidity providers, like market makers, possess significant market power. While we do not model dynamic limit order books in the paper, the presence of competing limit orders from customers does not alter our primary intuition, as long as there is imperfect competition among liquidity suppliers and thus some liquidity suppliers possess significant market power. For example, when the total number of buying orders and the total number of selling orders is significantly imbalanced, indicating a large order imbalance and insufficient competition from customer limit orders that aim to provide liquidity, market makers tend to have greater market power.

In most over-the-counter (OTC) markets, investors almost always trade with designated dealers (market makers) who typically provide bid and ask prices that adjust according to order sizes.8Ang, Shtauber, and Tetlock (2013)present evidence indicating that the illiquidity premium in OTC markets is several times higher compared to listed markets, implying that market makers in many OTC markets possess substantial market power. Moreover, the availability of Transaction Reporting and Compliance Engine (TRACE) information and electronic bond trading platforms effectively reduces search costs for both customers and dealers (Vames 2003;O’Hara and Zhou 2021;Goldstein and Hotchkiss 2007) find that the degree of price dispersion decreased after bond transactions were reported through TRACE, highlighting the impact of increased transparency.

These insights suggest that our model could potentially find application in some OTC markets where factors like bilateral negotiation, the need to search for a market maker, and price dispersion are of less critical importance. Examples could include OTCQX and OTCQB stock markets. The OTC Market Group facilitates electronic trading for both OTCQX and OTCQB, providing investors with real-time quoted bid and ask prices, along with comprehensive market data and depth.

In summary, our primary findings are relevant to markets characterized by imperfect competition among liquidity suppliers, particularly where some liquidity providers, such as market makers, have substantial market power. Our calibration results help validate the applicability of our model in markets featuring imperfectly competitive liquidity suppliers.

Studying trade dynamics is notoriously difficult because it typically requires a non-stationary dynamic model, which is challenging to solve in closed form.Afonso and Lagos (2015)provide a tractable framework for analyzing intraday dynamics in OTC markets with search and bilateral negotiations, specifically focusing on the intraday trade dynamics of the federal funds market. In contrast, our paper develops a tractable framework to analyze intraday trading dynamics for exchanges where order flows are endogenously determined, and liquidity suppliers can range from competitive to imperfectly competitive. This framework can be extended to explore important questions, such as the impact of information dynamics on trading and return dynamics within a non-stationary dynamic model.

Admati and Pfleiderer (1988)generate a U-shaped pattern of trading volume when liquidity traders concentrate their activity within specific time periods. However, their model suggests that trading costs are low during times of higher trading volume. This is in contrast to the findings ofFoster and Viswanathan (1993), who note that higher trading costs often correspond with increased trading volume, a prediction consistent with our model. In our model, intraday U-shaped patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spread can occur simultaneously.Brock and Kleidon (1992)show that the demand at open and close is greater and less elastic than at other times of the trading day due to periodic market closure and that this periodic demand generates high volume and concurrent wide spreads. In their model, buy and sell orders are Poisson distributed in time and the price process is exogenously assumed. In contrast, in our model, traders optimally choose in which period to trade and the optimal trading quantity within that period. The U-shaped patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spread arise endogenously because traders trade aggressively on their private information in early periods and postpone their trading due to hedging needs until later periods.Madhavan, Richardson, and Roomans (1997)develop and estimate a structural model of price formation. The estimated parameter values indicate that information asymmetry decreases throughout the day, while dealers’ execution costs rise. This result helps explain the U-shaped pattern in intraday bid-ask spreads. Different from our model, their structural model assumes a fixed order size and incorporates an exogenous cost for market makers when providing liquidity. As a result, their model cannot explain the concurrent U-shaped patterns in both bid-ask spreads and trading volume.

Bogousslavsky and Collin-Dufresne (2023)develop a continuous-time inventory model with exogenous order flows and explain the positive time-series relationship between the spread and volume for large stocks.Lee and Wang (Forthcoming)show that when an OTC market coexists with an exchange, traders who are more likely to be informed prefer to trade on the exchange. As a result, any variation in the relative mass of informed traders would induce a positive correlation between the bid-ask spread and the market share of the exchange. However, neither paper generates the double U-shaped intraday pattern in both the spread and the volume. Moreover, given that the volume spike toward market closure is driven by liquidity trades, Lee and Wang (Forthcoming) would predict a decrease in the bid-ask spread toward market closure.

Liu and Wang (2016)develop a one-period model of market making with asymmetric information and inventory risk, aiming to explain why bid-ask spreads may decrease with information asymmetry. In contrast, our paper focuses on the intraday dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread and sheds light on how information and liquidity shocks influence trading volume and bid-ask spread over time.Budish, Cramton, and Shim (2015)advocate for the use of frequent batch auctions as a solution to counteract the arms races in high-frequency trading. Our model focuses on discrete time trading without engaging in such arms races and traders in our model respond to commonly observed public information simultaneously.Lester et al. (2023)show that reducing trading or search frictions can potentially slow down dealers’ learning and can increase the bid-ask spread in the long run. Our paper differs from theirs as we focus on the dynamic patterns of bid-ask spread and trading volume without search frictions.
We consider a multiperiod model of trading that features a continuum of identical informed investors with mass N I ( I investors), a continuum of identical uninformed investors with mass N U ( U investors), and N M designated market makers ( M investors) who are also uninformed. Both informed and uninformed traders are perfectly competitive and market makers are imperfectly competitive. Traders can trade one risk-free asset and one risky security at trading periods t = 0 , 1 , … , T ⁠ . The risk-free asset, with a zero supply, serves as the numeraire, and thus the risk-free interest rate is normalized to zero. article.2 The Equilibrium:
In this section, we solve for the dynamic equilibrium bid and ask prices and trading volume in closed form. We focus on a piecewise linear equilibrium where the equilibrium trading prices can be expressed as a piecewise linear function of the state variables in the model. At period t , both informed investors’ private information v t and liquidity shock η t affect informed investors’ trading and thus the equilibrium prices. From market prices, other investors can only infer the value of the composite signal s t := v t − h t η t = V + ε t − h t η t , (10) where the coefficient h t will be determined below in Equation (12) . Thus both ask prices A t and bid prices B t are informationally equivalent to the composite signal s t , which is a linear combination of informed investors’ private signal and the amount of the nontradable risky asset. 2.1 The equilibrium with agency trading market makers

In this subsection, we study the case in which market makers only conduct agency trading, they maximize the expected profit from each round of trading and do not carry inventory over time. Specifically, at period t , given A t and B t , the optimal demand schedule of a type i investor ( ⁠ i ∈ { I , U } ⁠ ) is Θ t i ( A t , B t ) − Θ t − 1 i ( A t − 1 , B t − 1 ) = { P t i R − A t γ t i A t < P t i R , 0 B t ≤ P t i R ≤ A t , − B t − P t i R γ t i B t > P t i R , (13) where P t i R is the reservation price of type i traders at period t and will be presented in Equation (14) in Proposition 1 . In this subsection, we study the case in which market makers are risk neutral and engage in principal trading. They carry inventory over time and maximize their expected total profits. The market makers’ problem is presented inEquation (9). For tractability, we assume that uninformed traders are also risk neutral.16

At periodt, givenAtandBt, the optimal demand schedule of an informed investor is as presented inEquation (13), wherePtIRis the reservation price of informed traders at periodtand will be presented inEquation (26)inProposition 3below. Define the reservation price of market makers (⁠PtMR⁠) as a critical threshold. Market makers buy from (or sell to) informed traders if this threshold is higher (or lower) than the informed traders’ reservation price. By establishing this reservation price for market makers, we can present the results for the case with principal market makers in a manner parallel to the case with agency trading market makers.17Next, we provide a closed-form expression for the reservation prices of both traders and market makers in the subsequent proposition.

The coefficientμtis as defined inEquation (12)and the coefficientsξtI,ψtI⁠, andγtIfort<Tare all positive and can be computed recursively using Equation(A-73). The coefficientsξtM,ψtM⁠, andγtMfort<Tare all positive and can be computed recursively using Equation(A-79). Their values att=TareξTI=1,ξTM=0,ψTI=0,ψTM=1,γTI=λIoV,TI,andγTM=0.

AtT, an informed trader’s reservation price isPTIR=V^TI−μTXT−λIoV,TIθT−1I⁠. It increases in the composite signalV^TI−μTXTand decreases in the previous inventoryθT−1I⁠. In contrast, as inProposition 1, fort<T, the reservation price of informed traders increases in the composite signalV^tI−μtXtand is also influenced by the uninformed market makers’ estimationV^tU⁠. This is because, att<T, informed investors’ optimal demand consists of a long-term stock position and a short-term speculative position. While the long-term stock position is anchored to the informed investors’ composite signalV^tI−μtXt⁠, the short-term speculative position is derived from their predictions of trading prices in the upcoming period. Since the uninformed market makers’ estimate of the stock payoff affects the next period’s trading prices, the informed investors’ reservation price is naturally also influenced by market makers’ estimate of the fundamental valueV^tU⁠.

Likewise, the reservation price of risk-neutral market makers attis influenced by their estimate of the asset’s fundamental value,V^tU⁠, as well as the composite signalV^tI−μtXtand the previous inventory of informed traders,θt−1I⁠. It’s worth noting that, being risk-neutral, the market makers’ reservation price is unaffected by their own inventory levels.

article.3 The Intraday Dynamics of Trading Volum:
In this section, we study the intraday dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread. In our model, both trading volume and bid-ask spread might display U-shaped patterns over the trading duration. Additionally, we examine the impact of factors, such as market makers’ market power, trading rounds, information flows, and hedging demands on these intraday dynamics.

3.1 The impact of information and hedging on the intraday dynamics

To analyze the dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread, we first examine two distinct cases: one where trading is mainly driven by information and another where it is mainly driven by liquidity shocks. This approach allows us to highlight the contrasting impacts of information and hedging on the dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread.

For the case with agency trading market makers, trading volume at periodt(⁠Volt⁠) and bid-ask spread (⁠At−Bt⁠) are given in Equations (22) and (20) inProposition 2. For the case with principal trading market makers, trading volume and bid-ask spread are given in Equations (29) and(30).21

Figure 2, panels A and B, illustrates the dynamics of expected trading volume and bid-ask spread for both principal and agency market-making markets against trading duration in an information-driven market where information arrives every period and liquidity shock only arrives at the beginning to keep information from fully revealing.22As depicted inFigure 2, in an information-driven market, both the expected trading volume and bid-ask spread tend to decrease over time.23The intuition is simple. With more information arrivals over time, both uninformed investors and market makers become less uncertain about the fundamental value of the asset in later periods. The difference between the reservation prices of informed and uninformed traders becomes smaller and thus both trading volume and spread also tend to be smaller over time. The expected bid-ask spread and trading volume may experience a slight increase toward market closure (periodT) because market makers have market power and periodTis the last trading opportunity for traders.

Fig. 2 The dynamics of expected trading volume and spread against trading duration in information-driven markets Open in new tab Download slide

Figure 3, panels A and B, presents the dynamics of expected trading volume and bid-ask spread over the trading duration for both principal and agency market-making markets in a liquidity-driven market.24In this setting, liquidity shocks occur every period, while information arrives only at the beginning. As depicted in the right panel ofFigure 3, panels A and B, contrary to the case in an information-driven market, in a liquidity-driven market, except for the first several trading periods where trading also responds to the initial information, the bid-ask spread tends to increase over time. This rise in the bid-ask spread is attributed to the widening difference in reservation prices, a consequence of the arrival of liquidity shocks over time.

Fig. 3 The dynamics of expected trading volume and spread against trading duration in liquidity-driven markets Open in new tab Download slide

In scenarios with agency trading market makers—who do not carry inventory and aim to maximize round-by-round profits by offsetting trades with other traders—the trading volume exhibits a hump-shaped pattern in the middle, as illustrated in the left panel ofFigure 3, panel A. This pattern arises because traders, anticipating a wider bid-ask spread toward market closure, optimally shift more of their trading to earlier periods when bid-ask spreads are relatively lower to reduce total trading costs. Notably, our extensive numerical analyses consistently reveal a hump in the expected trading volume before market closure for cases with agency trading market makers, regardless of the presence or absence of new information arrivals, particularly when new liquidity shocks occur.

In contrast, with principal trading market makers, there is no hump-shaped pattern in the expected trading volume; instead, it tends to increase as market closure approaches.25This difference is primarily due to the objective of principal market makers to maximize total trading profits throughout the entire trading duration. They determine their optimal trading quantities (bid depth and ask depth) based on traders’ demand schedules at each trading period, which in turn dictate the trading prices. With the arrival of new liquidity shocks, the bid-ask spreads set by principal market makers increase more gradually toward market closure, rather than peaking drastically at market close, as observed with agency market makers. This difference is clearly illustrated by comparing panel A with panel B inFigure 3. Specifically, the expected bid-ask spread with agency trading market makers (Figure 3, panel A) increases tenfold fromt/T=0.6to market close att/T=1⁠, while the expected bid-ask spread with principal trading market makers (Figure 3, panel B) increases by only approximately 18% fromt/T=0.6to market close att/T=1⁠. Therefore, the relative levels of the expected bid-ask spreads in earlier periods, compared to those at market close, are much higher in the principal market maker case than in the agency market maker case.

This is because principal market makers, compared to their agency counterparts, optimally buy less from other traders at bid prices (resulting in lower bids) or sell less to other traders at ask prices (resulting in higher asks) in earlier periods. Consequently, the expected bid-ask spreads set by principal market makers in these earlier periods are higher than those in the agency market maker scenario. As a result, traders continue to spread out their trading over time but shift less of their trading to earlier periods. A larger volume of trading occurs toward market closure when market makers can also set wider spreads, as it is the last trading opportunity for traders. Therefore, the trading volume does not exhibit a hump-shaped pattern.

We now explore the dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread when informed traders access private information and experience liquidity shocks at constant rates. As illustrated inFigure 4, panels A and B, both trading volume and bid-ask spread display U-shaped patterns over the trading duration.26At the beginning and end of the trading duration, we observe higher trading volume and bid-ask spreads, while in the middle of the trading duration, both trading volume and bid-ask spreads are lower.

Fig. 4 The dynamics of expected trading volume and spread against trading duration with both information and liquidity shocks arriving at constant rates Open in new tab Download slide

The intuition is as follows. Even though traders tend to smooth out their trading due to market makers’ market power, they are inclined to act quickly on their private information since others might trade on the same information. They tend to postpone their trading due to hedging needs until later periods. This is because, with more information revealed through trading, information asymmetry decreases and thus trading cost due to the adverse selection effect becomes smaller over time. As a result, trading volume tends to be concentrated at market opening and closure.27Notably, the trading peak near market closure might be reached at a point prior to closure when market makers predominantly engage in agency trading. Since market makers with market power can charge wider bid-ask spreads when trading incentives are heightened, bid-ask spreads are wider at market opening and closure.

3.2 The effects of liquidity shock uncertainty and information precision

Next, we examine the impact of liquidity shock uncertainty and the precision of informed traders’ private information on the dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread. We assess how variations in these factors shape the magnitude of the U-shaped patterns in trading volume and bid-ask spread.

Figure 5, panels A and B, illustrates the dynamics of expected trading volume and bid-ask spread under two distinct liquidity shock uncertainties. The solid curve represents the case with an uncertainty of the liquidity shocks attwithτη,t−1=1/15⁠, while the dashed curve represents the case withτη,t−1=1/12⁠.28

As shown in the left panels ofFigure 5, panels A and B, andFigure 6, panels A and B, the U shape of trading volume becomes more pronounced when the uncertainty of liquidity shocks increases (ie, whenτη,tdecreases) or when the precision of informed traders’ private information is lower (ie, whenτε,tdecreases). This trend arises because uninformed traders may perceive that trades are more likely driven by hedging needs. Consequently, the adverse selection effect becomes less pronounced, allowing informed traders to trade more aggressively on their private information at market opening and shift more trading due to hedging demand at market close. As a result, the U-shaped pattern of trading volume becomes more pronounced. An empirical implication is that the U-shaped pattern of trading volume will be more prominent for stocks with less asymmetric information or a higher proportion of liquidity traders, assuming all other factors remain constant.

Similarly, the U-shaped pattern of the bid-ask spread becomes more pronounced with increased uncertainty of liquidity shocks (whenτη,tis lower), as demonstrated in the right panels ofFigure 5, panels A and B. When informed traders’ private information becomes more precise (keeping the magnitude of liquidity shocks constant), since informed traders tend to act quickly on their private information, the difference in reservation price at market opening becomes larger and it becomes smaller in later trading periods. Therefore, the expected bid-ask spread becomes wider at market opening and narrower during later trading periods when the precision of private information increases (ie, whenτε,tincreases), as illustrated in the right panel ofFigure 6, panels A and B.

3.3 The effects of trading rounds and market makers’ market power

In this subsection, we explore how variations in the number of trading rounds and the market power of market makers influence bid-ask spreads.

By adjustingT, we modify the number of trading rounds while keeping the time interval fixed at[0,1]⁠. This means traders can execute trades from time 0 to 1 acrossT+ 1 rounds in total (spanning trading periodst=0,1,…,T⁠). Additionally, while changing the number of trading rounds, we hold both the total precision of private information and the total uncertainty of liquidity shocks constant.

Figure 7depicts the dynamics of the expected bid-ask spread against the trading durationt/Tfor various combinations ofNM(indicating competitiveness among market makers) and the number of trading roundsT.30Both the total precision of private information and the total uncertainty of liquidity shocks are held constant. As illustrated inFigure 7, increasing either the trading frequency or the competition among market makers (ie, increasingNM) leads to a reduction in the bid-ask spread. The introduction of more trading rounds or additional market makers mitigates the market power of market makers, subsequently narrowing the bid and ask spreads. InFigure 7, it’s evident that the U-shaped pattern is more pronounced in less competitive markets (characterized by fewer market makers) or markets with fewer trading rounds. In such environments, market makers possess more market power, which allows them to set wider bid-ask spreads.

Fig. 7 The dynamics of expected bid-ask spread against trading duration for different N M and T for agency (left) and principal (right) trading market makers Open in new tab Download slide

An empirical implication of our model is that stocks with fewer liquidity suppliers, such as smaller stocks, and those with a pronounced order imbalance—giving liquidity suppliers market power—tend to exhibit a more significant U-shaped pattern in bid-ask spread.
article.4 Model Calibration and Implications:
Our main findings are applicable to markets characterized by imperfect competition among liquidity suppliers. As an illustrative example, we perform model calibration using stocks listed on NASDAQ.

The expected payoff of the asset (⁠V¯⁠) has minimal influence on the expected bid-ask spread and turnover. Therefore, we setV¯to 1. We also setτV=1because, in our model, what’s pivotal are the relative magnitudes of the precision of payoff, information, and liquidity shocks. We normalize the total supply of the asset,θ¯⁠, to 1. As a result, the expected trading volume in our model corresponds to the average turnover, which is empirically used to measure trading volume. To calibrate our model, we aim to find model parameters that can match the theoretical expected absolute bid-ask spreads and cumulative turnover to the corresponding empirical averages of these measures at half-hour intervals throughout the trading day. For this purpose, we setT= 13 in our model calibration.33To further reduce the number of free parameters, we assumeNM= 1 andNI=1.5⁠, indicating that the population of informed traders is 1.5 times that of the market makers.34We assume that the risk aversion of uninformed traders,λU, is set to one for the agency trading market-making case, and to zero in the principal trading market-making case. We will calibrate the risk aversion of informed traders accordingly.

There remain six parameters in our model, which include the mass of uninformed traders (NU), the risk aversion of informed traders (λI), the precision of information at market opening (⁠τε,0⁠), the precision of information at other periods (⁠τε,tfor0<t≤T⁠), the precision of liquidity shocks at market closure (⁠τη,T⁠), and the precision of liquidity shocks at other periods (⁠τη,tfor0≤t<T⁠). As suggested by our model’s intuition, the magnitude of the U-shaped intraday pattern of the bid-ask spread is determined by the precision of private information, the precision of liquidity shocks, and the competition level among market makers.

Calibrating model parameters to simultaneously match both the bid-ask spread and trading volume across all trading points is highly challenging because of the need to align 27 data points. Therefore, we calibrate the model parameters to match the empirical averages of absolute bid-ask spreads observed at eight key trading times–9:30, 10:30, 11:30, 12:30, 13:30, 14:30, 15:30, and 16:00–and the empirical cumulative trading volume at seven trading times–10:00, 11:00, 12:00, 13:00, 14:00, 15:00, and 16:00.

The calibrated parameter values indicate that the precision of private information is higher at market opening compared to other periods, while the uncertainty of hedging needs is greater at market closure. 4.2 Market makers’ exogenous inventory shocks and traders’ execution costs

Although a dynamic setting where risk-averse market makers simultaneously perform both agency and principal market making would not be tractable in our paper, we explore a one-period version of our model to investigate the effects of inventory shocks from risk-averse market makers on other market participants’ trading costs.Figure 10depicts the expected ask price, bid price, and competitive price in two scenarios: one where market makers possess a significantly large positive position (panel A) and another where market makers hold a significantly negative position (panel B).35

Fig. 10 Expected ask price (solid curve), expected bid price (dashed curve), and expected competitive price (dot-dashed curve) against σ X Open in new tab Download slide

InFigure 10, panel A, shows that when market makers have a notably positive initial endowment, the expected ask price closely aligns with the competitive price. Meanwhile, panel B illustrates that the expected bid price remains close to the competitive price when the initial endowment is significantly negative. This suggests that with a large stock inventory, market makers offer better prices to buyers, and with a substantial short position, they provide more favorable prices to sellers.

These findings are in line with empirical research byBogousslavsky, Collin-Dufresne, and Sağlam (2021), who examine the impact of exogenous shocks to the inventory of a designated market maker for a set of NYSE stocks on market liquidity and other market participants’ execution costs. They find strong evidence indicating that traders who trade on the “same side” as the market makers consistently experience higher execution costs compared to “opposite side” trades.
article.5 Conclusion:
In this paper, we present a dynamic trading model based on flows of private information and liquidity shocks with imperfectly competitive liquidity suppliers. The market power of market makers leads other traders to smooth out their trading, even though they are not strategic. Traders are motivated to trade on their private information in earlier periods to capitalize on potential profits, as other traders might also act on similar information. However, they delay their trading due to hedging needs to later periods, as the adverse selection effect becomes less severe over time. Consequently, trading volume may exhibit a U-shaped pattern, with higher trading volume observed at the beginning and the end of the trading duration and lower volume in the middle. The market power of liquidity suppliers enables them to widen bid-ask spreads when traders’ trading incentives are heightened, resulting in wider bid-ask spreads at market opening and closure. Therefore, our model provides a unified and coherent explanation for the observed U-shaped patterns in both trading volume and bid-ask spread.

Our model explains the empirical finding that liquidity demanders pay most of the bid-ask spread while nondealer liquidity suppliers get much more favorable prices from market makers. Our model predicts that the variation in trading volume tends to be smaller for stocks with less competitive liquidity suppliers during periods of news events. The U-shaped pattern of the bid-ask spread tends to be more pronounced for stocks with less competitive liquidity suppliers (e.g., small stocks) and stocks with higher order imbalance. The U-shaped pattern of the trading volume tends to be more pronounced for stocks with lower information asymmetry or a higher proportion of liquidity traders. The model’s calibration aligns with the empirical intraday patterns of bid-ask spread and trading volume.

Therefore, we focus on calibrating model parameters that align with the theoretical expected absolute bid-ask spreads to match the corresponding empirical averages of absolute bid-ask spreads.

33 In our model, without new information or hedging needs, market makers keep their quoted bid and ask prices unchanged. Traders, in these intervals, make a transaction based on the optimal quantity determined by their prevailing information and hedging demand. When new information or hedging needs arise, traders adjust their optimal quantities and market makers update the quoted prices accordingly. The dynamics of trading volume and bid-ask spread when information or liquidity shocks arrive sporadically is illustrated in Section B.5 of the Internet Appendix . Practically speaking, during market opening, there might be more than 14 trading rounds. But, given the infrequent arrival of new signals and changes in hedging needs throughout the day, our calibration assumes T = 13 (14 trading rounds).
